query: infertility causes with healthy uterus and ovaries

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Fertilization -- Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Infertility is a common problem encountered in the medical community. If a female patient cannot become pregnant after one year of unprotected intercourse performed regularly, an evaluation is in order. An important consideration is the patient’s body mass index. If a patient is overweight, weight loss may improve her chances of becoming pregnant. [11] It is essential to consider labs in patients who are unable to become pregnant because abnormalities of the thyroid gland or androgen excess may indicate an endocrinopathy. A commonly encountered endocrinopathy that causes issues with fertility is polycystic ovarian syndrome. In PCOS, testosterone increases, which interferes with egg maturation. [12] A physical exam is also useful in evaluating infertility. Masses or tenderness in the adnexae or the pouch of Douglas is consistent with endometriosis or chronic pelvic inflammatory disease. Furthermore, structural abnormalities may suggest an infection, leiomyoma, malignancy, or Mullerian anomaly. In women who are not suspected to be ovulating, it is crucial to do a thorough hormone analysis to investigate if ovulation is occurring. A mid-luteal progesterone level should be tested one week before expected menstruation. To show proof of ovulation, a progesterone level greater than 3 ng/mL is the expectation. Another useful hormone to evaluate female fertility is the anti-Mullerian hormone. [13] This hormone is in the TGF-beta family and expresses itself by small early antral follicles. These levels reflect the size of the primordial follicle pool and are a good indicator of ovarian function.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Female Reproduction -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical relevance of female reproductive physiology is broad. Understanding relevant physiology is important when diagnosing and treating disorders of the female reproductive system. Several diseases, disorders and medical managements of the female reproductive system are mentioned below. However, this list is not exhaustive, and in-depth details are out of the scope of this article. Bacterial vaginosis Vaginal candidiasis Sexually transmitted infections Pelvic inflammatory disease Vaginitis Bartholin gland cyst Ovarian cysts Cervical, ovarian, endometrial cancer Contraception (eg, IUD, oral, implant, condoms) Assisted reproductive techniques for infertility Gonadal dysgenesis, 45,X (Turner Syndrome) Abnormal uterine bleeding Amenorrhea Polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS) Premenstrual syndrome (PMS) Endometriosis Asherman Syndrome Ovarian torsion Bicornate uterus [20] [25] [26] [27] [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Female Infertility -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The 5 diagnostic evaluation categories are: Semen analysis Assessment of ovarian function and reserve Assessment of the uterine cavity Assessment of the fallopian tubes Endocrinological serum studies

retrieved_docs.title: Female Infertility -- Pathophysiology -- Endometriosis
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis is defined as endometrial tissue outside the uterine cavity. The diagnosis is based on the histological identification of endometrial glands or stroma outside the uterus. Endometriosis is most commonly found in the pelvis but can spread throughout the entire abdomen and affects 10% to 15% of reproductive-age women. [19] Of women with endometriosis, 40% to 50% experience infertility. [20] Endometriosis is categorized into 4 stages, according to the American Society of Reproductive Medicine, with stage I being minimal and stage IV severe. Endometriosis is known to cause infertility, but the pathophysiology is thought to change according to the stage. [21] For stages I and II, infertility is believed to be associated with inflammation and increased production of prostaglandins and cytokines, macrophages, and natural killer cells. [22] The inflammation impairs ovarian and tubal function, resulting in defective follicular formation, fertilization, and implantation. [23] Stages III and IV are associated with pelvic adhesions or masses that distort pelvic anatomy; this inherently impairs tubal motility, oocyte release, and sperm motility. [24] Also, advanced endometriosis is hypothesized to impair folliculogenesis, reducing fertilization potential. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Female Reproduction -- Pathophysiology -- Infertility
retrieved_docs.content: Fecundability is the ability to conceive. A successful pregnancy requires a complex sequence of events, including ovulation, fertilization, and implantation into a viable uterine cavity. The probability of conceiving during timed intercourse (ovulation) is approximately 16% to 28%, though percentages can vary slightly between studies. [22] Infertility is defined as the failure to achieve pregnancy after 1 year (12 months) of regular, unprotected sexual intercourse. [23] Primary infertility is designated to individuals who have never conceived, while secondary infertility applies to those who have conceived in the past but are now experiencing infertility. Either female factors, male factors, or both can cause infertility. The most common female factors leading to infertility, in decreasing order, include ovulatory disorders, endometriosis, pelvic adhesions, tubal blockage, other uterine/tubal abnormalities, and hyperprolactinemia. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography Gynecology Infertility Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Technique or Treatment -- Transvaginal Ultrasound
retrieved_docs.content: Baseline assessment includes evaluation of uterus, endometrium, ovaries, and cul de sac. Uterus: The uterus is evaluated in both sagittal and transverse planes (Figure 4). Attention is given to evaluate the size, shape, orientation of the uterus, cervix, myometrium, and endometrium. The length and height of the uterus are measured in the sagittal plane, and width is measured in transverse or coronal planes at the level of the tubal ostia or cornual regions. The myometrium is evaluated for any pathology like fibroids or adenomyosis. The location and character of all pathological lesions are noted and documented in the images and the final report. Endometrium - The endometrium is measured perpendicularly from one echogenic to the other echogenic border at its thickest part in the midsagittal plane (Figure 5). If the fluid is visualized in the cavity, the endometrium is measured by combining the anterior and posterior endometrial thicknesses separately without including the fluid. Adnexa: The adnexa is evaluated for any masses such as hydrosalpinges, para-ovarian cysts, or para-tubal cysts. Ovaries: The ovaries are identified bilaterally and measured in three dimensions (length, height, and width) (Figure 6). The antral follicles are seen as round or oval anechoic structures. A baseline AFC is performed by counting the number of follicles between 2 to 10 mm and measuring the dimensions of follicles greater than or equal to 10 mm in the early follicular phase in both ovaries. [14] AFC can be measured using three different techniques during the transvaginal scan; a traditional real-time two-dimensional (2D) scan, stored 2D cine loops, or three-dimensional (3D) data sets. Real-time 2D scanning is used to measure the ovary in both the longitudinal and coronal planes. The next step is to optimize the ultrasound settings for better image quality while centering the ovary in its largest axis. The ovary should occupy at least 50% of the screen while counting the follicles ranging from 2 to 10 mm from one ovarian margin to the other. The total follicle number for both ovaries is added to obtain AFC. [10] The 2D cine loop uses the same technique as real-time 2D ultrasound except that the loop involves the continuous sweep of the ovary. The data is saved and assessed at a later time. Even though 3D ultrasound has better reproducibility than 2D methods, it needs additional special training and ultrasound equipment. The technique to perform 3D ultrasound is beyond the scope of this basic topic hence will not be discussed in detail. Cul de sac: The cul de sac is evaluated for any fluid, and a sliding sign is performed to assess for any adhesions. The examiner's hand is placed on the patient's abdomen to apply pressure on the uterus. The transvaginal probe is placed near the posterior vaginal fornix to assess whether the cervix and the uterus glide over the anterior rectum and sigmoid colon. The lack of sliding of these organs implies adhesive disease of these structures. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: In Vitro Fertilization -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 10% to 15% of couples will have difficulties getting pregnant.  Infertility is defined as no conception after 12 months of unprotected sexual intercourse in women less than 35 or six months in women 35 years or older. [3] If the female partner has oligomenorrhea or amenorrhea, endometriosis, tubal disease, or there is a known male issue, an earlier evaluation is indicated. [4] The workup for couples experiencing infertility includes an assessment of ovulatory function, ovarian reserve, uterine cavity, tubal patency, and a semen analysis.  If there is evidence of endometriosis, tubal occlusions, or adnexal adhesions then a diagnostic laparoscopy should be considered.

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography Gynecology Infertility Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Understanding the different phases of the endometrium during the menstrual cycle and normal developmental phases in the ovarian follicles is essential for both initial baseline screening assessment and monitoring during natural, IUI, or IVF cycles.

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography Gynecology Infertility Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Ovary
retrieved_docs.content: Based on the stages of development, the ovaries have primordial, primary, secondary, tertiary, or antral follicles. The number of primordial follicles determines true ovarian reserve. Primordial follicles are small and difficult to be seen by ultrasound. The ovary has 6-7 million primordial follicles at around five months of gestational age, slowly declining to approximately 1000 at menopause. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography Gynecology Infertility Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Ovary
retrieved_docs.content: With the initiation of menarche, a cohort of dormant primordial follicles are recruited during each day of the menstrual cycle, and these follicles grow in size steadily. These primordial follicles develop into primary, secondary, and antral follicles. Development of the fluid-filled cavity, also called the antrum adjacent to the maturing oocyte, results in forming of the antral or tertiary follicle. In normal cycles, one follicle matures and is destined to ovulate while other follicles degenerate and ultimately disappear through a process called apoptosis or programmed cell death.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Uterus -- Pathophysiology -- Endometriosis
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis is a chronic and painful gynecologic disease that typically presents with pelvic pain, AUB, dysmenorrhea, and/or dyspareunia. Dyschezia (pain with defecation) can occur if endometrial tissue is implanted in the pouch of Douglas. If the intestines or bladder are involved, abdominal pain and painful urination will occur, respectively. Symptoms typically vary with hormone changes and often get worse during menstruation. The inflammation associated with endometriosis has the potential to damage or scar the fallopian tubes and ovaries, leading to infertility. On physical exam, the uterus will be normal in size.

retrieved_docs.title: Female Infertility -- Pathophysiology -- Uterine Causes
retrieved_docs.content: Uterine causes of infertility are associated with either space-occupying lesions or reduced endometrial receptivity. In regards to uterine leiomyomas (fibroids), 1 meta-analysis demonstrated that only submucosal or intracavitary fibroids impaired implantation and pregnancy rates compared to other infertile controls. [31] Congenital uterine abnormalities (CUA), although rare, are also associated with infertility. Most commonly found are uterine septums, which are also associated with recurrent pregnancy loss. Interestingly, one study demonstrated that the prevalence of CUA in the fertile and infertile population is the same. [32] Infertility due to CUA is thought to account for roughly 8% of the female causes of infertility; however, 25% of women with late first-trimester or second-trimester miscarriages are found to have CUAs. [33] A detailed discussion of the classifications and pathophysiology of CUAs is outside the scope of this manuscript.

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Amenorrhea -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Regular menstruation depends on a complex sequence of events consisting of hormone production leading to ovulation, the effects of hormonal fluctuations from ovulation and the absence of fertilization on the endometrium, and an anatomically normal reproductive tract where this physiologic process can occur. The basic requirements for normal menstrual cycles include four anatomically and functionally distinct structural components: the hypothalamus, anterior pituitary gland, ovary, and the genital outflow tract composed of the uterus, endometrium, cervix, and vagina. If any of these components are nonfunctional or abnormal, menstrual bleeding may not occur; however, the precise pathophysiology associated with amenorrhea may vary based on the underlying etiology and sometimes may be unclear. [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Female Infertility -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The World Health Organization (WHO) performed a large multinational study to determine gender distribution and infertility etiologies. In 37% of infertile couples, female infertility was the cause; in 35% of couples, both male and female causes were identified; in 8%, there was male factor infertility. [3] In the same study, the most common identifiable factors of female infertility are as follows: Ovulatory disorders: 25% Endometriosis: 15% Pelvic adhesions: 12% Tubal blockage: 11% Other tubal/uterine abnormalities: 11% Hyperprolactinemia: 7%

retrieved_docs.title: Assisted Reproductive Technology (ART) Techniques -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: For the female evaluation, day 3 follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), estradiol (E2), antral follicle count, and Anti Mullerian Hormone (AMH) are determined via bloodwork and baseline transvaginal ultrasound to evaluate ovarian reserve. Further medical evaluation in patients with ovulatory dysfunction includes collections of thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH), prolactin, DHEAS, testosterone, and 17 hydroxy-progesterone.  The above hormonal assessment helps determine the etiology of anovulation. In addition, the uterine and pelvic cavity is also typically evaluated with a baseline transvaginal ultrasound, often in conjunction with a hysterosalpingogram or sonohysterogram. The ultrasound evaluation helps to identify any uterine factors affecting fertility and pregnancy maintenance, such as the presence of polyps, submucosal fibroids, and/or uterine malformations such as uterine septums.

retrieved_docs.title: Amenorrhea -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Regular menstruation depends on a complex sequence of events consisting of hormone production leading to ovulation, the effects of hormonal fluctuations from ovulation and the absence of fertilization on the endometrium, and an anatomically normal reproductive tract where this physiologic process can occur. The basic requirements for normal menstrual cycles include four anatomically and functionally distinct structural components: the hypothalamus, anterior pituitary gland, ovary, and the genital outflow tract composed of the uterus, endometrium, cervix, and vagina. If any of these components are nonfunctional or abnormal, menstrual bleeding may not occur; however, the precise pathophysiology associated with amenorrhea may vary based on the underlying etiology and sometimes may be unclear. [7] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2535
retrieved_docs.content: Female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) and endometrial implantation (انتباذ بطاني رحمي) are interconnected medical conditions. Prevention and control measures are applicable to both female infertility and endometrial implantation, and the two conditions are related, indicating that endometrial implantation can contribute to or be associated with female infertility and that managing endometrial pathology is important in the care of women with infertility.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) is closely related to endometrial implantation (انتباذ بطاني رحمي). Abnormalities in the process of endometrial implantation can impair the uterine lining’s ability to support embryo attachment, thereby contributing to infertility in women. Recognizing the connection between these two conditions is important for clinicians when evaluating reproductive health and planning appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2427
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammatory diseases of the female pelvic organs are identified as having the ovary (ovarium) as their finding site, indicating that the ovary is the primary location where such pelvic inflammatory conditions manifest. Likewise, any ovary problem is described as having its finding site in the ovary itself, reinforcing the ovary’s central role in the pathology of disorders that originate in this organ. In addition, the ovary (ovarium) is noted to be the anatomical location for an ovarian pregnancy (حمل مَبيضِيّ), highlighting that ectopic implantation of a gestational sac can occur within the ovary. Together, these relationships emphasize the ovary’s significance as both the site of inflammatory pelvic disease, the focal point for ovary‑specific problems, and a possible location for an ovarian pregnancy, all of which are important considerations in diagnosis and management of female pelvic health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 730
retrieved_docs.content: Infertility in women of cervical origin is identified as a specific disorder of the cervix (اضطراب في عنق الرحم) and represents a subtype of female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث). Female infertility, in turn, is closely linked to intrauterine adhesions (انتباذ بطاني رحمي) and may arise as a consequence of inflammation of the uterine lining (الْتِهابُ بِطانَةِ الرَّحِمِ). These interconnections indicate that cervical pathology, uterine lining inflammation, and intrauterine adhesions can all contribute to the development of female infertility, underscoring the importance of evaluating cervical health, endometrial status, and the presence of adhesions when assessing and managing infertility in women.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2154
retrieved_docs.content: Disorder of fallopian tube and ovary and noninflammatory disorder of ovary are gynecologic conditions that share the ovary (ovarium) as their finding site, indicating that the ovary is the primary anatomical location examined when these disorders are evaluated. In addition, the ovary (ovarium) is identified as the location involved in حمل مَبيضِيّ (ovarian pregnancy), an ectopic implantation of a gestation within the ovary. Together, these relationships emphasize that the ovary serves as a common site for both noninflammatory ovarian pathology and for ovarian ectopic pregnancies, highlighting the need for careful assessment of the ovary in patients presenting with related symptoms or reproductive concerns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1719
retrieved_docs.content: Infertility in women of cervical origin is classified as a specific type of cervical disorder (اضطراب في عنق الرحم). This condition is a subset of the broader category of female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث). Female infertility is closely related to endometrial implantation abnormalities (انتباذ بطاني رحمي), indicating that disturbances in the uterine lining can contribute to the inability to conceive. Radiotherapy (radioterapie) is recognized as an approved therapeutic option for cervical disorders and for endometrial implantation abnormalities; in certain clinical scenarios where these pathologies underlie female infertility, radiotherapy may be considered as part of the management strategy. Understanding the hierarchical relationship between cervical‑origin infertility, general female infertility, and endometrial implantation, as well as the role of radiotherapy, helps clinicians tailor comprehensive treatment plans for patients experiencing infertility.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 4 -- Community 2764
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis is a gynecological disorder characterized by the presence of endometrial‑like tissue that is normally located in the uterus, and its ectopic growth can cause pain, inflammation, and fertility issues. Understanding that endometriosis originates from tissue associated with the uterus is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted treatment, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 5 -- Community 2938
retrieved_docs.content: Female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) and adenomyosis (انتباذ بطاني رحمي) are both gynecologic conditions for which epidemiological information is available, highlighting the importance of population‑level data in understanding their prevalence and risk factors. Epidemiology provides insight into how common each disorder is and which groups are most affected. Moreover, adenomyosis is directly related to female infertility, indicating that the presence of adenomyotic tissue within the uterine wall can contribute to difficulties conceiving. Recognizing the epidemiological patterns of both conditions and their interrelationship aids clinicians in identifying at‑risk patients, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing management strategies aimed at improving reproductive outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 188
retrieved_docs.content: Consequently, comprehensive gynecologic assessment and targeted interventions are required to address menstrual irregularities, preserve organ integrity, and manage the interconnected vascular, structural, and hormonal factors influencing female reproductive health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2812
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammatory diseases of female pelvic organs are typically found in the ovary (ovarium). Lesions of the ovary and general ovary problems are likewise identified with the ovary as their finding site. In addition, the ovary can serve as the anatomical location for an ovarian pregnancy (حمل مَبيضِيّ). Recognizing the ovary as the common site for these inflammatory conditions, lesions, and ectopic pregnancies supports accurate diagnosis and focused patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 908
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammatory diseases of female pelvic organs, lesions of the ovary, and noninflammatory disorders of the ovary all share the ovary (ovarium) as their finding site, indicating that the ovary is the primary location for these diverse pathologic processes. In addition, the ovary can serve as the anatomical site of an ovarian pregnancy (حمل مَبيضِيّ), an ectopic gestation that occurs within the ovarian tissue. Together, these relationships underscore the central role of the ovary in both inflammatory and non‑inflammatory pelvic conditions as well as in rare pregnancy complications, highlighting the need for careful evaluation of ovarian involvement in female pelvic health and disease management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1880
retrieved_docs.content: According to the finding_site_of relationship, inflammatory diseases of female pelvic organs are identified with a finding site in the ovarium. In a similar manner, a Lesion of ovary is described as having a has_finding_site in the ovarium. The ovarium itself is linked by a has_finding_site to اضطراب مبيضي (an ovarian disorder) and by a has_location to حمل مَبيضِيّ (an ovarian pregnancy). These interconnected statements highlight that inflammatory conditions, lesions, and specific disorders of the ovary are all centered on the ovary as the primary anatomical site, and they may be associated with rare presentations such as ovarian pregnancy, emphasizing the importance of recognizing the ovary’s role in diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1532
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are uterine conditions that arise within the uterus and are clinically associated with optional termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري). This relationship underscores that these complications are localized to the uterine environment and may influence or be influenced by decisions regarding elective pregnancy termination, highlighting the importance of uterine health in the management of pregnancy‑related problems.

The infectious process is identified as the underlying mechanism of post‑abortion complications, linking infection directly to adverse outcomes after termination procedures. Collectively, this structured framework interrelates prenatal surveillance, invasive diagnostics, therapeutic interventions, delivery assistance, placental and ectopic‑pregnancy management, and post‑delivery care, providing a comprehensive reference for clinical documentation, education, and patient‑centered obstetric management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are clinically associated with إنهاء الحمل – اختياري (optional termination of pregnancy) and also have a relationship with اضطراب رحمي (uterine disorder). This indicates that uterine disorders are considered among the other complications of pregnancy and that optional termination of pregnancy can be clinically linked to these pregnancy‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2692
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotherapy is recognized as an allowable therapeutic option for both female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) and uterine endometriosis (انتباذ بطاني رحمي). These two gynecologic conditions are interrelated, as uterine endometriosis is a known contributor to infertility in women. Consequently, radiotherapy may be considered within the broader management strategies for these linked disorders, reflecting their clinical connection and the potential role of radiotherapeutic interventions in addressing both conditions.
